name: Committed Action
tagline: Take a small step toward your values even when you don't feel ready.

origin.source: Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (Hayes, Strosahl & Wilson, 1999)

situations: Knowing what matters but not doing it
summary: Committed Action is the doing half of ACT: choosing behavior guided by your values rather than your mood. You define a small, concrete step and take it whether or not the feelings cooperate. Over time these steps build patterns of effective action that don't depend on motivation.
howItWorks: Most people wait for feelings to change before they act, but in ACT the order is reversed — action shapes feeling, not the other way around. By linking a tiny behavior to a value you care about, you bypass the need for motivation and build momentum. Acceptance of discomfort runs alongside: you make room for the reluctance instead of waiting for it to disappear.

steps.instruction: Name the value this connects to (e.g. 'being a present parent', 'doing honest work').
Choose one step so small you're almost certain you can do it today.
Make room for any discomfort that shows up — let it be there without obeying it.
Take the step now, then pick the next one.
steps.note: Repetition is the point; one step is a proof of concept, not the goal.

whenToUse: When you know your direction but aren't moving
When motivation has failed you repeatedly
Breaking a large goal into livable pieces
When feelings keep vetoing your intentions
whenNotToUse: When the 'value' is actually someone else's expectation
If steps are set so large they guarantee failure
When genuine rest or recovery is what's actually needed
tips: Make the first step laughably small — shrink it until it feels easy
Tie it explicitly to a value, not an outcome
Track steps, not results — consistency is the win
Expect discomfort; its presence isn't a sign to stop

evidence.summary: Committed action is a core process of ACT, which is supported by numerous randomized trials and meta-analyses across anxiety, depression, and chronic pain.
citations: Hayes, S.C., Strosahl, K. & Wilson, K.G. (1999). Acceptance and Commitment Therapy. Guilford Press.
A-Tjak, J.G. et al. (2015). A meta-analysis of ACT. Psychotherapy and Psychosomatics.
